segments.title: HMS Dreadnought's 12-inch starting point
segments.summary: The guide uses HMS Dreadnought's 12-inch guns as the visual baseline for later battleship-calibre comparisons.
segments.body: The opening establishes the size of a 12-inch dreadnought gun by comparing its bore diameter with a human head. That concrete scale helps make later increases in calibre more intelligible than a list of nominal measurements alone. It also makes clear why the 12-inch weapon is the natural starting point for a guide to dreadnought-style capital ships.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces dreadnought gun calibres and identifies Dreadnought's 12-inch gun as the first comparison.

segments.title: The step from 12 to 13.5 and 14 inches
segments.summary: The Orion-class 13.5-inch gun shows that a seemingly modest increase in bore size still mattered in capital-ship armament.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places the Orion-class 13.5-inch weapon beside the 14-inch class of guns to show how close the two look. The comparison is a useful corrective to treating calibre labels as interchangeable: even a small change in bore diameter affected the weapon a navy chose for its battleships. This moment gives a physical sense of the incremental escalation in pre-First World War gun size.

evidence.note: The transcript compares Orion's 13.5-inch gun with a 14-inch gun and says the difference still matters.

segments.title: Queen Elizabeth's 15-inch calibre
segments.summary: A chest-width comparison conveys the imposing diameter of the Queen Elizabeth class's 15-inch main battery.
segments.body: The 15-inch gun of the Queen Elizabeth class is presented as nearly as wide as the speaker's chest. The visual comparison turns a familiar calibre into an immediate impression of the shell's scale. It is a useful watch point for understanding why the 15-inch gun became such a formidable benchmark in British battleship armament.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Queen Elizabeth class's 15-inch guns and compares the shell width with a chest.

segments.title: Colorado's 16-inch gun in human scale
segments.summary: The Colorado class's 16-inch guns push the visual comparison beyond a human head, illustrating the next jump in heavy-gun scale.
segments.body: At 16 inches, the guide moves from a broad visual comparison to the suggestion that two heads could fit within the gun's diameter. The point is not a ballistic calculation, but a memorable sense of how quickly battleship main batteries grew. It gives viewers a practical reference for the American 16-inch calibre before the final jump to 18 inches.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Colorados' 16-inch guns and uses a two-head comparison for their diameter.

segments.title: Yamato and the 18-inch extreme
segments.summary: Yamato's 18-inch guns close the guide with a shoulder-width illustration of the largest calibre in the comparison.
segments.body: The final comparison places the 18-inch guns associated with Yamato at roughly shoulder-to-shoulder width. This is the guide's visual endpoint: the jump from the early dreadnought 12-inch baseline to the largest practical battleship calibres. Opening here gives viewers a quick way to grasp why Yamato's armament remains synonymous with extreme capital-ship firepower.

evidence.note: The transcript names Yamato among the ships with 18-inch guns and compares the diameter with shoulder width.
